children.name: Ardea
children.description: Great herons and egrets — the largest and most familiar members of the family. The grey heron (gråhäger) is one of the most recognisable birds in Sweden: a tall, slate-grey sentinel standing motionless at the water's edge for hours, then striking with lightning speed to impale a fish with its dagger bill. Grey herons nest colonially in tall trees ('heronries') — colonies in Swedish parks and forests can be centuries old and contain hundreds of pairs. The great white egret (ägretthäger) was once so rare in Sweden that a sighting was a national event; following a dramatic European range expansion it now breeds in southern Swedish marshes and is increasingly common as a winter visitor in Stockholm.

children.name: Ardea cinerea
children.description: The gråhäger — Sweden's most familiar large wading bird. Present year-round, haunting the edges of every Swedish lake, river, and coastal waterway. Breeding colonies (heronries) in treetops can be enormous and noisy, with birds returning to the same colony for decades. Can stand motionless for hours before striking with a stabbing lunge.

children.name: Ardea alba
children.description: The ägretthäger — one of Sweden's most dramatic recent colonists. Bred for the first time in Sweden at Kvismaren in 1998; now established across southern Sweden. Its elegant white plumage and black bill make it unmistakeable. The American egret was hunted almost to extinction for its breeding plumes in the 19th century — its recovery catalysed the modern conservation movement.

children.name: Ardea purpurea
children.description: Slimmer and more rufous than the grey heron with a snake-like neck and dark streaked plumage. A scarce but annual visitor to Swedish wetlands, breeding occasionally in southern Swedish reedlands. More secretive than grey heron — hides in dense reedbed when alarmed, standing upright with neck stretched to mimic a reed stem.

children.name: Ardea herodias
children.description: The largest heron in North America, standing nearly a metre tall with blue-grey plumage and a white face adorned with a dark plume. Found along virtually any body of water across North America, from Alaskan tidal flats to Yucatán mangroves. During breeding season, adults develop elaborate plumes and their bills turn orange-yellow.

children.name: Ardea cocoi
children.description: The South American counterpart to the Great Blue Heron, with similar size and slate-grey plumage but a distinctively black-and-white head pattern. Inhabits rivers, lakes, and coastal wetlands throughout South America east of the Andes. A solitary and patient hunter, standing motionless in shallow water before striking swiftly at fish.

children.name: Ardea pacifica
children.description: A large, long-legged heron endemic to Australia with a striking white neck and head contrasting with dark grey upperparts. Nomadic in habits, following rainfall across the continent to exploit temporary wetlands in arid regions. One of Australia's most widespread herons, adapting readily to farm dams and flooded paddocks.

children.name: Ardea melanocephala
children.description: A large African heron distinguished by its contrasting black crown and nape against a white throat and foreneck. Unlike most herons it is often found far from water, stalking grasslands and farmland for rodents, large insects, and small reptiles. One of the most abundant large herons in sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Ardea humbloti
children.description: A large, dark grey heron restricted to Madagascar and the Comoro Islands, where it inhabits coastal wetlands, estuaries, and inland lakes. Named after the French naturalist Léon Humblot, who collected natural history specimens in the western Indian Ocean in the 1880s. Classified as Vulnerable due to habitat loss and hunting pressure.

children.name: Ardea insignis
children.description: One of the world's rarest herons, restricted to remote rivers and wetlands in the eastern Himalayas from Bhutan through Assam to Myanmar. This very large, slate-grey heron with a white belly is Critically Endangered with probably fewer than 250 individuals remaining. Its decline is driven by disturbance at nesting sites and degradation of large riverine habitats.

children.name: Ardea sumatrana
children.description: The largest heron in Southeast Asia and Australasia, with a massive dagger-like bill, grey-brown plumage, and a preference for coastal mangroves, tidal mudflats, and remote tropical islands. Found from the Andaman Islands east through the Indonesian archipelago to New Guinea and northern Australia. A solitary and wary bird rarely seen far from saltwater.

children.name: Ardea intermedia
children.description: A medium-sized, all-white egret intermediate in size between the Great White Egret and the Little Egret, with a shorter, blunter bill and no black on its feet. Widespread across Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and Australia in freshwater wetlands, paddyfields, and flooded grasslands. Develops yellowish-buff pectoral plumes during the breeding season.

children.name: Ardea goliath
children.description: The world's largest heron, standing up to 152 cm tall with a wingspan exceeding 185 cm and rich chestnut-and-grey plumage. Inhabits large lakes, rivers, and coastal lagoons in sub-Saharan Africa and locally in South Asia. Specialises in large fish that other herons cannot handle, striking with a bill powerful enough to pierce substantial prey.

children.name: Ardea bournei
children.description: A large, very poorly known heron endemic to Santiago Island in the Cape Verde archipelago, described from a small number of specimens. Named after the ornithologist W. R. P. Bourne who first collected it in 1954. Considered Vulnerable and subject to ongoing taxonomic debate, sometimes treated as a colour morph of the Purple Heron. Restricted to cliff-edged ravines on a single island.

children.name: Ardea novaehollandiae
children.description: A medium-sized, richly coloured heron of Australia and the Pacific with warm rufous-buff upperparts, a black crown, and white underparts — historically placed in Ardea but increasingly treated under Notophoyx novaehollandiae in some modern classifications. Found in mangroves, forest streams, and wetlands across Australia to Micronesia. The 'nankeen' colouring refers to its warm orange-buff tones, resembling the Chinese cotton fabric of the same name.

children.name: Bubulcus
children.description: Cattle egrets — stocky white herons that have achieved one of the most remarkable natural range expansions in ornithological history. Originally confined to Africa and southern Asia, cattle egrets colonised South America by the early 20th century, spread through the Americas to North America by the 1950s, and have since established themselves on every inhabited continent. They follow large grazing animals — or tractors and lawn mowers — to catch invertebrates flushed by their movements.

children.name: Bubulcus ibis
children.description: A compact white egret that associates closely with large grazing animals, riding on cattle backs and catching insects disturbed by hooves. The most abundant heron in the world. In breeding plumage adults develop buff-orange wash on head, breast, and back. An occasional vagrant to Scandinavia.

children.name: Bubulcus coromandus
children.description: The Asian and Australasian counterpart of the Western Cattle Egret, recently recognised as a separate species based on breeding plumage differences — the Eastern develops more extensive orange-buff coloration on the head, neck, and back. Widespread from India through Southeast Asia to Australia, where it has expanded its range substantially since the 1940s.

children.name: Butorides
children.description: Striated herons and their allies — small, compact herons with dark, streaked plumage that inhabit dense vegetation along waterways worldwide. These are among the most widespread herons, with representatives on every continent except Antarctica. They are famously resourceful hunters, occasionally using bait — dropping feathers, insects, or bread onto water — to lure fish within striking range, one of very few documented tool-use behaviours in birds.

children.name: Butorides striata
children.description: A small, dark heron with green-glossed upperparts and a streaked neck found from West Africa through Asia to Australia and South America. The most subspecies-rich heron, reflecting its vast range across tropical and subtropical wetlands. Well known for using bait to attract fish to within striking range.

children.name: Butorides virescens
children.description: A small, stocky heron of North and Central America with rich chestnut neck, dark green back, and yellow-orange legs that become bright orange in breeding season. One of the few birds documented to use tools — dropping bait onto water surfaces to attract fish. Common in wooded swamps, streams, and pond edges.

children.name: Butorides sundevalli
children.description: An all-dark, sooty-grey heron endemic to the Galápagos Islands, where it hunts along lava shorelines and tidal pools. Named after the Swedish zoologist Carl Jakob Sundevall. Remarkably tame, it has evolved little fear of humans in its predator-free island environment, making it easy to observe at close range.

children.name: Ardeola
children.description: Pond herons — small, squat herons of the Old World tropics, notable for their cryptic brown streaked plumage when at rest and their startlingly white wings revealed in flight. Six species divide the tropical Old World from West Africa through Madagascar and South Asia to Southeast Asia. They are birds of rice paddies, marshes, and vegetated pond margins, where their mottled resting plumage makes them nearly invisible until they take wing.

children.name: Ardeola ralloides
children.description: A compact buff-brown heron of Mediterranean and African wetlands that appears startlingly white in flight when its wings are revealed. A summer visitor to southern Europe that winters in tropical Africa. Occasionally overshoots to northern Europe in spring. Breeds colonially in trees or reedbeds alongside other herons and egrets.

children.name: Ardeola grayii
children.description: The most familiar small heron of the Indian subcontinent, found in virtually every wetland from roadside ditches and temple ponds to large marshes and rice paddies. Perfectly camouflaged at rest, it erupts from underfoot in a blaze of white wings. Named after the English naturalist John Edward Gray.

children.name: Ardeola bacchus
children.description: The pond heron of China and Southeast Asia, breeding in the east and wintering across a broad area from Myanmar to the Greater Sundas. In breeding plumage the head and neck are washed deep chestnut-maroon. A common bird of rice paddies, fish ponds, and shallow freshwater margins throughout lowland Asia.

children.name: Ardeola speciosa
children.description: The pond heron of the Malay Peninsula and the Indonesian archipelago, distinguished in breeding plumage by a bright orange-buff head and neck. Common in lowland wetlands throughout Indochina, the Malay Peninsula, and Indonesia. Its range overlaps and intergrades with Chinese Pond Heron in some areas.

children.name: Ardeola idae
children.description: An Endangered pond heron that breeds only in Madagascar and the Aldabra Atoll, wintering on the East African mainland from Kenya to Mozambique. Unlike its relatives it is almost entirely white in breeding plumage, resembling a small white egret. The global population is thought to number only a few thousand individuals.

children.name: Ardeola rufiventris
children.description: A small, dark pond heron of sub-Saharan Africa with a dark slaty-grey head and back and distinctive rufous-chestnut underparts. Inhabits densely vegetated freshwater wetlands, marshes, and flooded grasslands. Much less commonly observed than the Indian or Chinese pond herons due to its secretive habits among tall emergent vegetation.

children.name: Egretta
children.description: Typical egrets and reef herons — a diverse genus of mostly small to medium white or dark herons found on every continent. The genus spans all habitats from mountain streams to coral reefs, and includes species with both white and dark colour morphs. Several were decimated by the 19th-century plume trade, which drove demand for their elaborate breeding aigrettes used in women's hats; their recovery sparked the founding of the Audubon Society and early conservation movements.

children.name: Egretta garzetta
children.description: A slender, all-white egret with a black bill and legs and distinctive yellow feet. Has expanded dramatically into northern Europe since the 1990s and now breeds regularly in Britain and occasionally in Scandinavia. Hunts actively, running and foot-stirring to flush prey, unlike the more static grey heron. Develops two long scapular plumes in breeding season.

children.name: Egretta thula
children.description: The North American counterpart to the Little Egret, with identical white plumage, black bill and legs, and bright yellow feet. Was nearly extirpated in the late 19th century for its breeding plumes, and its protection helped establish the National Audubon Society. Now one of the most abundant herons in the Americas.

children.name: Egretta eulophotes
children.description: A medium-sized white egret breeding on offshore islands of eastern China and Korea and wintering in the Philippines and Borneo. Vulnerable due to habitat loss and disturbance at its restricted breeding sites on rocky islands. Distinguished from Little Egret in breeding season by its yellow bill, blue facial skin, and shaggy crest plumes.

children.name: Egretta vinaceigula
children.description: A small, dark egret restricted to the Okavango Delta and Cuando–Zambezi system of southern Africa, with dark grey plumage, a vinous-red throat patch, and yellow-green feet. Vulnerable due to its tiny global range and dependence on seasonal floodplain wetlands. Often associates with Rufous-bellied Herons in flooded grasslands.

children.name: Egretta picata
children.description: A strikingly pied egret of Australia and New Guinea with a slate-black body and white neck and head, and a blue-black crest in breeding plumage. Inhabits freshwater and estuarine wetlands across northern Australia and southern New Guinea. Gregarious, often nesting in large mixed colonies with other herons and cormorants.

children.name: Egretta sacra
children.description: A reef heron occurring in both white and dark grey colour morphs, found on rocky coastlines, coral reefs, and mangrove margins from the Andaman Islands across the Pacific to Polynesia. One of the most maritime herons, spending its entire life along coastlines and rarely venturing inland. White and dark morphs may pair together.

children.name: Egretta rufescens
children.description: A medium-sized egret of Caribbean and Gulf Coast tidal flats that occurs in dark reddish-brown and white morphs. Renowned for its spectacular feeding behaviour — dancing, spinning, and spreading its wings like an umbrella to create shade that attracts fish. Restricted to saltwater habitats year-round.

children.name: Egretta caerulea
children.description: A medium-sized heron of the Americas with slate-blue adult plumage and a distinctive maroon-purple neck and head. Immatures are white, resembling Snowy Egrets, and gradually acquire blue feathers through a dappled intermediate stage. Common in freshwater and estuarine marshes from Maine to Peru.

children.name: Egretta tricolor
children.description: A slender heron of the Americas with dark blue-grey upperparts, purple and maroon accents, and a conspicuous white belly and thighs. Highly active hunter, chasing prey across tidal mudflats and shallow lagoons with rapid running pursuit. One of the most abundant herons of Gulf and Caribbean coastlines.

children.name: Egretta gularis
children.description: A reef heron of the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, and West African coast occurring in dark grey and white morphs. Closely related to the Pacific Reef Heron, it inhabits coastal rocks, mangroves, and tidal mudflats, rarely moving inland. The dark morph is more common in West Africa while the white morph predominates in the Arabian Peninsula.

children.name: Egretta dimorpha
children.description: An East African reef heron of Madagascar and nearby island groups (Comoros, Seychelles, Aldabra) occurring in both dark grey and white morphs. Long treated as a subspecies of Western Reef Heron but now recognised as a distinct species based on molecular and morphological evidence. Inhabits rocky coastlines, reefs, and mangrove channels.

children.name: Egretta ardesiaca
children.description: An all-black African egret with yellow feet renowned for its extraordinary 'canopy feeding' technique — forming a full umbrella with its wings over its head to create a patch of shade that attracts fish. The shade also reduces glare, making prey easier to spot. Widespread in sub-Saharan Africa in shallow freshwater and coastal wetlands.

children.name: Egretta novaehollandiae
children.description: Australia's most common heron, instantly recognised by its blue-grey body and brilliant white face and throat. Ubiquitous across Australia and New Zealand in almost any wet habitat from farm dams to urban parks and tidal estuaries. Highly adaptable, feeding on frogs, insects, small reptiles, and crustaceans as well as fish.

children.name: Agamia
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the spectacular Agami Heron of Central and South American rainforest streams. It is among the most brilliantly coloured of all herons, with iridescent green, chestnut, and silver plumage, and a remarkably long, slender bill adapted for hunting small fish in fast-flowing, shaded forest streams.

children.name: Agamia agami
children.description: One of the most spectacular herons in the world, with iridescent dark green and chestnut upperparts, a rich chestnut chest, and long silvery ornamental plumes developing in breeding season. Inhabits shaded forest streams and swamps in lowland tropical America from southern Mexico to Bolivia and Brazil. Hunts by standing motionless in dark forest streams and striking fish with its extremely long bill.

children.name: Pilherodius
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Capped Heron, a medium-sized, strikingly patterned heron of South American river margins. It bears a unique combination of a black cap, white or creamy-buff body, and a long blue facial lore, unlike any other heron in its range.

children.name: Pilherodius pileatus
children.description: A medium-sized heron with a distinctive black cap, creamy-white body, and vivid blue facial skin at the lore, found along slow-moving rivers and forest streams in lowland South America east of the Andes. Generally solitary and inconspicuous, perching low in riparian vegetation. The blue loral skin becomes more vivid during courtship.

children.name: Cochlearius
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Boat-billed Heron — a distinctive, nocturnal heron of Neotropical mangroves and forest waterways whose enormous, boat-shaped bill is unique among all wading birds. The bill is thought to be adapted for scooping prey from soft mud or shallow water in low-light conditions.

children.name: Cochlearius cochlearius
children.description: A nocturnal heron of Neotropical mangroves, forest streams, and brackish lagoons with an extraordinarily broad, scooped bill unlike that of any other heron. The enormous bill is used to scoop shrimp, fish, frogs, and large insects from the water. Roosts communally by day in dense mangrove or gallery forest and becomes active at dusk.

children.name: Tigrisoma
children.description: Tiger-herons — large, heavily barred or streaked herons of tropical American forest rivers and streams. The intricate tiger-striped pattern provides superb camouflage in dappled forest light. They are among the most handsome herons in the world, with rich rufous, black, and buff markings, and inhabit the banks of fast-flowing lowland and foothill rivers.

children.name: Tigrisoma lineatum
children.description: The most widespread tiger-heron, found along forest streams and river margins from Honduras to northern Argentina. Adults are rich rufous with fine black-and-white barring on the back and wings; immatures are broadly barred brown and buff. Stands motionless at the water's edge for long periods, then strikes fish with a rapid lunge.

children.name: Tigrisoma fasciatum
children.description: A tiger-heron of fast-flowing rocky rivers and mountain streams from Costa Rica to Bolivia and Argentina, replacing the Rufescent Tiger-Heron at higher altitudes. More finely barred than its relatives, with grey-brown upperparts and bold black-and-white neck streaks. Listed as Near Threatened due to habitat degradation along Andean river systems.

children.name: Tigrisoma mexicanum
children.description: The northernmost tiger-heron, ranging from Mexico through Central America to Colombia, notable for its extensive bare yellow-green facial and throat skin. Inhabits river margins, mangroves, and coastal lagoons at low elevations. Like all tiger-herons, immatures are broadly barred and take several years to acquire adult plumage.

children.name: Tigrisoma salmoni
children.description: A very poorly known tiger-heron restricted to the Pacific slope of Colombia and possibly northwestern Ecuador, inhabiting dense forest streams and swamp-forest edges. Named after the 19th-century British collector Thomas Salmon who collected specimens in Colombia. Sometimes treated as a subspecies of the Rufescent Tiger-Heron.

children.name: Tigriornis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the White-crested Tiger-Heron, an enigmatic and little-studied forest heron of West and Central African rainforest rivers. Distinguished from the American tiger-herons by its white crest and confined to the humid lowland forests of the Congo Basin and West African rainforest belt.

children.name: Tigriornis leucolophus
children.description: A large, secretive heron of West and Central African rainforest streams with intricate black-and-rufous barring on the body and a distinctive white crest raised when alarmed. Little studied due to its dense, remote forest habitat. Inhabits shaded, slow-moving streams and swampy forest interiors of the Congo Basin and Upper Guinean forests.

children.name: Zebrilus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Zigzag Heron, the smallest of the tiger-pattern herons and one of the least-known birds in the world. Restricted to Amazonian forest streams in South America, it is so secretive and rare in collections that little is known of its behaviour or ecology.

children.name: Zebrilus undulatus
children.description: One of the world's least-known birds, a tiny heron restricted to the interior of Amazonian rainforest where it haunts the edges of small forest streams. Its intricate zigzag-patterned rufous and black plumage provides camouflage on the forest floor. Seldom observed, and its vocalisation — a distinctive repetitive call — is often the only clue to its presence.

children.name: Ixobrychus
children.description: Little bitterns — the smallest herons, barely larger than a blackbird. The little bittern (dvärghäger) is a scarce and elusive breeding species in the southernmost Swedish reedlands. Like its larger relative, it freezes with bill pointing skyward when disturbed, relying on its cryptic streaked plumage to disappear among reed stems. Males have black backs and wings with large buff wing patches that flash conspicuously in flight. Little bitterns are nocturnal hunters, creeping through dense reed stems to catch small fish, frogs, and invertebrates. Their call — a soft, rhythmic 'wup-wup-wup' — is one of the quieter and more easily overlooked sounds of a Swedish reedbed.

children.name: Ixobrychus minutus
children.description: The dvärghäger — Europe's smallest heron. A scarce summer visitor to dense Swedish reedlands in Skåne and Öland. Creeps through reeds like a mouse. Male has black and buff wing pattern; female is brown and streaked. A sighting is a genuine prize for Swedish wetland birders.

children.name: Ixobrychus sinensis
children.description: The most common little bittern of East and Southeast Asia, with warm buff-yellow plumage and dark flight feathers that create a striking wing pattern in flight. Found in reedbeds, rice paddies, and dense marsh vegetation from Pakistan east to Japan and south to Australia. Pumps its body rhythmically when alarmed, mimicking swaying reed stems.

children.name: Ixobrychus cinnamomeus
children.description: A uniformly warm cinnamon-rufous little bittern of South and Southeast Asia, inhabiting reedbeds, tall grass, and marshy thickets from Pakistan through India to the Philippines and Indonesia. The male is entirely rich cinnamon, making it one of the most uniformly coloured herons. Active at dawn and dusk in dense marsh vegetation.

children.name: Ixobrychus sturmii
children.description: Africa's only little bittern, a small, dark slate-grey heron with buff underparts streaked with brown, found in dense reedlands, papyrus swamps, and flooded grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa. Named after the German naturalist Johann Heinrich Christian Friedrich Sturm. A secretive species more often heard than seen. Named after Johann Sturm.

children.name: Ixobrychus involucris
children.description: A small South American bittern with bold buff and dark brown streaking on the upperparts and a striped buff underside, found in marshy grasslands and reedbed fringes from Colombia and Venezuela south to central Argentina. The least studied of the American little bitterns. Secretive and most active at night in dense tall grass and rushes.

children.name: Ixobrychus exilis
children.description: The smallest heron in North America, barely larger than a meadow vole, with rich chestnut and buff plumage and large buff wing patches. Creeps through tall marsh reeds grasping multiple stems simultaneously with its large feet, rarely flying unless flushed. Found in freshwater and brackish marshes from Canada through the Caribbean to South America.

children.name: Ixobrychus novaezelandiae
children.description: An extinct little bittern known from subfossil bones and a small number of late 19th-century specimens from New Zealand's North and South Islands. Its disappearance was likely caused by introduced mammalian predators following Polynesian and European settlement. The last confirmed record dates from 1900.

children.name: Ixobrychus dubius
children.description: Recently split from the Little Bittern as a distinct Australasian species, with subtle plumage differences and a disjunct range in eastern Australia and possibly New Guinea. Inhabits reedbeds, sedge marshes, and flooded grasslands in eastern Australia. Difficult to observe, more often detected by its repetitive low booming call at dusk and dawn.

children.name: Ixobrychus flavicollis
children.description: The largest of the Ixobrychus bitterns, with blackish upperparts and a distinctive yellow neck stripe on both sides, found from India and Sri Lanka through Southeast Asia to Australia. Despite its name, it is not truly black but dark slate-grey. Inhabits well-wooded streams, mangroves, and forest ponds, more arboreal in behaviour than other bitterns.

children.name: Ixobrychus eurhythmus
children.description: A migratory little bittern of East Asia, breeding in reedbeds and wet grasslands in Manchuria, Korea, and Japan and wintering in Southeast Asia and the Greater Sundas. Named after the German explorer Leopold von Schrenck who studied the Amur River region. A poorly known species due to its secretive habits in dense marsh vegetation.

children.name: Ixobrychus payesii
children.description: A small, dark bittern of West and Central African forest zones with dark grey upperparts and rufous underparts streaked white, inhabiting the dense margins of forest streams, swamp-forest, and papyrus wetlands. Named after the French naturalist Ernest Payé. Rarely observed and poorly known, with most records from forest-edge habitats during the rains.

children.name: Ixobrychus seychellarum
children.description: A Critically Endangered little bittern endemic to the Seychelles Islands, where the last confirmed sightings were made in the 1940s and the species is likely extinct. It inhabited freshwater marshes and streams on the main granite islands. Its extinction was caused by wetland drainage and the introduction of predatory mammals.

children.name: Botaurus
children.description: Bitterns — cryptically camouflaged, reedbed-dwelling herons with one of the most extraordinary vocalisations in the animal kingdom. The great bittern's 'boom' — a deep, resonant foghorn-like sound carrying up to 5 km — is produced by inflating specialised air sacs in the oesophagus and is most often heard at dawn and dusk in early spring. Bitterns are masters of camouflage: their brown, streaked plumage matches reedbeds perfectly, and when alarmed they stretch upward with bill pointing skyward, swaying gently to mimic reeds in the wind. The Swedish rördrom is a thinly distributed and declining breeding bird, requiring extensive reedlands that are being lost to drainage and water level management.

children.name: Botaurus stellaris
children.description: The rördrom — one of Sweden's most atmospheric and elusive birds. Its booming call, audible at 5 km on still spring mornings, is one of the most arresting sounds in Swedish nature. Requires large reedlands; a declining breeding bird in Sweden. Birds increase their booming frequency dramatically in cold springs when ice delays breeding.

children.name: Botaurus lentiginosus
children.description: The North American counterpart of the Eurasian bittern. Its pumping call ('thunder pumper') is a defining sound of North American marshes. An occasional vagrant to western European coastlines and British wetlands in autumn. Slightly smaller and brighter than its Eurasian relative.

children.name: Botaurus pinnatus
children.description: A large bittern of wet grasslands and marshes from Mexico through Central America to Brazil and Bolivia, with boldly streaked brown and buff plumage. Like the American Bittern it produces a booming or pumping call in the breeding season. Favours open wet savannas and flooded grasslands rather than dense reedbeds, making it more visible than its relatives.

children.name: Botaurus poiciloptilus
children.description: The bittern of Australia and New Zealand, Endangered and declining due to wetland drainage and reed management. Larger and darker than the American Bittern, with rich brown and buff streaking and a loud booming call heard at night and at dawn in freshwater reedbeds. Secretive year-round, detected mainly by its distinctive foghorn-like boom from dense vegetation.

children.name: Syrigma
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Whistling Heron, a slender, pale heron of South American open grasslands and cerrado that is unique in the family for preferring dry savanna habitats well away from water. Its melodious whistling call — unusual among herons — gives it its common name.

children.name: Syrigma sibilatrix
children.description: A medium-sized pale heron with soft grey, buff, and chestnut plumage and a distinctive melodious whistle given in flight. Found in open grasslands, cerrado, and campo habitats across central and eastern South America from Venezuela to Argentina, often far from open water. Frequently encountered singly on dry pasture and savanna, quite unlike the wetland habits of most herons.

children.name: Nycticorax
children.description: Night herons — stocky, large-headed herons active primarily at dusk and night. The Black-crowned Night Heron is one of the world's most widespread herons, nesting colonially on every continent except Australia and Antarctica. Its evocative 'quock' call, delivered in flight at dusk, is a classic sound of evening wetlands across the Northern Hemisphere. Night herons roost in dense vegetation by day and begin hunting fish, frogs, and invertebrates as darkness falls.

children.name: Nycticorax nycticorax
children.description: The world's most widespread heron, found on every continent except Australia and Antarctica. A stocky, hunch-shouldered bird with a black crown and back, grey wings, and a white underside. Roosts gregariously in trees by day and emerges to hunt fish, frogs, and invertebrates from dusk onwards. Occasionally overshoots to Scandinavia in spring.

children.name: Nycticorax caledonicus
children.description: The Australasian counterpart of the Black-crowned Night Heron, with rich rufous-brown plumage, a black crown, and long white head plumes in breeding season. Found across the Philippines, Wallacea, New Guinea, and Australia in mangroves, forest streams, and wetlands. More strongly nocturnal than the Black-crowned Night Heron; roosts in dense mangrove or riparian trees by day.

children.name: Nyctanassa
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Yellow-crowned Night Heron of the Americas, closely related to Nycticorax but distinguished by its striking black-and-white head pattern with a yellow crown, and its specialisation on crustaceans — particularly crabs and crayfish — rather than fish.

children.name: Nyctanassa violacea
children.description: A stocky night heron of the Americas with a boldly patterned black-and-white head, yellow crown, and grey body, specialising on hard-shelled crustaceans including crabs and crayfish. Common in coastal mangroves, freshwater swamps, and wooded streams from Kansas and New England south to Peru and Brazil. More diurnal than the Black-crowned Night Heron.

children.name: Gorsachius
children.description: Forest night herons — four secretive, heavy-bodied night herons of Asian and African forest zones. These are among the least-known herons in the world, inhabiting dense forest streams and mountain ravines where they are rarely observed. Several species were described from very few specimens and have not been reliably observed in the wild for decades.

children.name: Gorsachius melanolophus
children.description: A large, richly patterned night heron with a black crest, chestnut body, and heavily barred underparts found in foothill and montane forests from India and Sri Lanka through southern China to the Philippines. Strictly nocturnal, roosting by day in dense forest undergrowth near streams and ravines. The most widespread of the forest night herons.

children.name: Gorsachius magnificus
children.description: A Critically Endangered night heron known from only a handful of specimens and a very small number of recent photographic records, restricted to montane forest streams in southern China and Vietnam. Almost nothing is known of its ecology or behaviour. Its extreme rarity may partly reflect its remote, steep-sided forest-ravine habitat rather than genuine scarcity.

children.name: Gorsachius leuconotus
children.description: An African forest night heron with dark brown plumage and a distinctive white patch on the back, found along heavily shaded forest streams and wooded rivers from West Africa to East Africa and south to Zambia. Strictly nocturnal and exceptionally secretive, much more often heard — giving a deep croaking call — than observed. Sometimes placed in Calherodius.

children.name: Gorsachius goisagi
children.description: An Endangered night heron breeding only in the broadleaved forests of Japan's main islands and wintering in the Philippines. A stocky, rufous-brown bird with bold black streaking on the neck, it inhabits forested slopes near streams. Severely threatened by deforestation in its breeding range; the total population may number only a few hundred pairs.

children.name: Zonerodius
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Forest Bittern of New Guinea — an enigmatic, very poorly known heron with tiger-like barring that inhabits lowland and foothill forest streams in New Guinea. Rarely collected and seldom observed, it remains one of the least-studied herons in the world.

children.name: Zonerodius heliosylus
children.description: An enigmatic bittern of New Guinea's lowland and lower montane rainforests with heavily barred rufous and dark brown plumage reminiscent of tiger-herons. Known from fewer than 20 specimens; its behaviour, ecology, and voice are essentially unknown. Inhabits dense forest near slow-moving streams and swampy forest interiors in Papua New Guinea and Irian Jaya.
